narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive strengths like your outstanding undergraduate GPA of 3.90—well above the typical 3.21 average—and a solid LSAT score of 37, right around the usual 36.70 mark, along with your upper-middle family income background. You're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for most students at 92.7%. However, the admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 45.1%, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize applicants with lower predicted risks. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, far below the average of about 5.5, suggesting your later academic performance in school raised concerns about sustaining success through law school and the bar. On a positive note, your exceptional undergrad GPA strongly pushed the model toward expecting you to pass, counteracting some worries. Your family income level also helped, nudging the prediction in a favorable direction compared to lower brackets. Your LSAT score contributed positively too, aligning well with successful students. Meanwhile, your Year 1 decile of 1—below the average 5.7—added a smaller push toward the failure prediction, though your full-time status barely influenced things either way. Overall, while your early strengths shine, the dip in your later school grades stood out as the most influential red flag when compared to peers who typically rank higher in those deciles and pass the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a brilliant Black woman ready to break barriers in law—it's just one data-driven snapshot. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting those later academic rankings, perhaps through targeted coursework or recommendations highlighting your growth, and consider retaking the LSAT if possible. Keep pushing forward; your high GPA and determination position you well for another shot at success.
